title: mmap in podman container returns ENODEV on MacOS Host
body:
### Issue Description

I'm trying to use cache files in a podman container. The cache files are shared across multiple `podman run` command. I used `mmap` to map the cache file into memory. That works fine on Linux host, but fails on MacOS host, with `ENODEV` error.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Consider the following Dockerfile and script. This script is supposed to compile and run a simple C program that uses mmap to map a file into memory. The file is mounted into the container using a volume mount.

1. Create a directory and put the Dockerfile and script in it.
`Dockerfile`
```dockerfile
FROM fedora:latest

RUN dnf -y install gcc \
    && dnf clean all
```

`run.sh`
```bash
#!/bin/bash

set -xeu

podman build -t test .

MOUNT_DIR=$(pwd)/mountdir

mkdir -p ${MOUNT_DIR}

cat <<EOF > ${MOUNT_DIR}/test.c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/mman.h>

int main(int argc, char *argv[]) {
    if (argc != 2) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Usage: %s <file>\n", argv[0]);
        exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
    }

    const char* input_file = argv[1];

    int result = 0;
    int fd;
    size_t size;
    void *addr;

    FILE* fp = fopen(input_file, "r");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        perror("fopen");
        result = EXIT_FAILURE;
        goto cleanup;
    }

    fd = fileno(fp);
    if (fd == -1) {
        perror("fileno");
        result = EXIT_FAILURE;
        goto cleanup;
    }

    if (fseek(fp, 0, SEEK_END) == -1) {
        perror("fseek");
        result = EXIT_FAILURE;
        goto cleanup;
    }

    size = ftell(fp);
    if (size == -1) {
        perror("ftell");
        result = EXIT_FAILURE;
        goto cleanup;
    }

    addr = mmap(NULL, size, PROT_READ, MAP_SHARED, fd, 0);
    if (addr == MAP_FAILED) {
        perror("mmap");
        result = EXIT_FAILURE;
        goto cleanup;
    }

    printf("size: %zu\n", size);
    printf("addr: %p\n", addr);

    if (munmap(addr, size) == -1) {
        perror("munmap");
        result = EXIT_FAILURE;
    } else {
        result = EXIT_SUCCESS;
    }

cleanup:
  if (fp != NULL) {
    fclose(fp);
  }

  return result;
}
EOF

podman run --rm -v ${MOUNT_DIR}:/mnt test bash -c "gcc -o /mnt/a.out /mnt/test.c && /mnt/a.out /mnt/test.c"
```

2. Run the `run.sh` script

### Describe the results you received

The script works fine on Linux, but fails on MacOS with the following error:
```
mmap: No such device
```
which means `ENODEV`.

### Describe the results you expected

Returns a valid address and size. For example, on Linux, the output is:
```
size: 1313
addr: 0x7f8a3333c000
```

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Host OS: MacOS 13.5

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body:
Sounds normal, if you are trying to use it on the network file system?

	
`ENODEV`

_The underlying filesystem of the specified file does not support memory mapping._

You need to use a local disk instead, then it should work like on Linux
Closing as I think @afbjorklund is right on spot.

@afbjorklund  Um, no. I don't use a network file system. I use a local disk. Upon seeing what logs or explanations did you conclude that I use a network file system?

It might be due to MacOS; as far as I understand, Podman uses QEMU to run the container. Is that the reason?
For the reference, I didn't put the label `remote` on this issue.

> It might be due to MacOS; as far as I understand, Podman uses QEMU to run the container. Is that the reason?

Yes, on the Mac the `podman` is actually `podman-remote` and talks to the CoreOS VM with a default $HOME mount

What was the value of MOUNT_DIR (or pwd) ?

And what filesystem is mounted, on that path ?

> Yes, on the Mac the `podman` is actually `podman-remote` and talks to the CoreOS VM with a default $HOME mount
> 
> What was the value of MOUNT_DIR (or pwd) ?
> 
> And what filesystem is mounted, on that path ?


My Mount dir is not a special one; it is just a directory in my home directory (`/Users/leebc/src/test_podman_mount`).

The filesystem of the Host is APFS.

When I run a podman container with mount, I can check the mount point in the container, and it is a 9p filesystem.

```
$ cat /proc/mounts | grep /mnt
vol0 /mnt 9p rw,relatime,access=client,msize=131072,trans=virtio 0 0
```

I encountered this issue and found that the 9p file system supports the `cache` option. (https://www.kernel.org/doc/Documentation/filesystems/9p.txt)

I modified the podman code to add `cache=mmap` option to the mount command.

https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/62a22c5d6098e51b6b113fa29dd8f35a0c336fb1/pkg/machine/qemu/machine.go#L482
```diff
--- a/pkg/machine/qemu/machine.go
+++ b/pkg/machine/qemu/machine.go
@@ -479,7 +479,7 @@ func (v *MachineVM) mountVolumesToVM(opts machine.StartOptions, name string) err
                case MountType9p:
                        mountOptions := []string{"-t", "9p"}
                        mountOptions = append(mountOptions, []string{"-o", "trans=virtio", mount.Tag, mount.Target}...)
-                       mountOptions = append(mountOptions, []string{"-o", "version=9p2000.L,msize=131072"}...)
+                       mountOptions = append(mountOptions, []string{"-o", "version=9p2000.L,msize=131072,cache=mmap"}...)
                        if mount.ReadOnly {
                                mountOptions = append(mountOptions, []string{"-o", "ro"}...)
                        }
```

And it works as expected. I also found that the `cache=5` option is present in the container's `/proc/mounts` file.
```
$ cat /proc/mounts | grep /mnt
vol0 /mnt 9p rw,relatime,cache=5,access=client,msize=131072,trans=virtio 0 0
```

Note that the number `5` means `CACHE_SC_MMAP` in the kernel source code (https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v6.4.11/source/fs/9p/v9fs.h#L59).

So, the issue becomes whether we can add the `cache=mmap` option to the mount command or not. Should I open a new issue for this?

I'm trying to run prometheus on a mac with a mounted local volume:

```
podman machine init -v /Users/rrc:/Users/rrc
podman machine start
podman run -v "$PWD/promdata:/prometheus" prom/prometheus
```

Could this be related?

```
ts=2023-09-07T12:11:03.901Z caller=query_logger.go:93 level=error component=activeQueryTracker msg="Error opening query log file" file=/prometheus/queries.active err="open /prometheus/queries.active: permission denied"
panic: Unable to create mmap-ed active query log
```

`podman run -v $PWD/promdata:/prometheus ubuntu touch /prometheus/queries.active`  succeeds, so it seems I have normal write access to the file, but can't open it mmap'd.
